user.description: Set up Necromancer. Add the devices you want guarded afterwards.

init.title: PoE ports

add_port.title: PoE port
add_port.description: A PoE port: how it's switched, how the connected device is recognised, how its online status is checked, and its timing.

power.name: Power switch
power.description: Switch that cuts power (PoE) on the port (e.g. a smart plug or PoE switch).

identity.name: Recognition
identity.description: How Necromancer knows which device is on this port — an entity that reports it, or a fixed value (e.g. MAC, IP or name).

data.id_static: Or a fixed value (if there's no entity)

status.name: Online status
status.description: How Necromancer sees whether the port is online — to confirm the power-cycle worked (e.g. a ping or reachability sensor).

timing.name: Timing
timing.description: Wait times while powering the port off and on.

edit_port.title: Which port to edit?
edit_port.description: Pick the port you want to edit.

delete_port.title: Which port to delete?
delete_port.description: Pick the port you want to remove.

import_ports.title: Import ports
import_ports.description: Paste a list of ports as YAML. “Merge” adds new ports and updates existing ones with the same name; “Replace” overwrites the whole list. Tip: export first to see the exact format.

export_ports.title: Export ports
export_ports.description: Choose which ports to export — all are selected by default.

export_result.title: Exported ports
export_result.description:
Copy the YAML below — edit it and paste it back via “Import ports”.

```
{yaml}
```

error.no_self_link: Necromancer's own devices can't be linked. Pick a different device.
error.duplicate_name: A guard with this name already exists. Pick a unique name so the status entities stay unambiguous.
error.action_required: This recovery needs an action — a guard with no action can only escalate.

user.title: State source
user.description: How should Necromancer tell whether the device is OK — from an entity's state, or from a Jinja template?

reconfigure.title: State source
reconfigure.description: How should Necromancer tell whether the device is OK — from an entity's state, or from a Jinja template?

device.title: Device & state
device.description: Give the device a name and define how its state is detected and what happens on a problem.

state_check.name: State check
state_check.description: How Necromancer tells whether the device is OK (e.g. a ping or reachability sensor). Leave the attribute empty to use the entity's state; any value other than "OK"/"faulty" counts as unknown and triggers nothing.

template_check.name: State template
template_check.description: A Jinja template that returns true (OK) or false (faulty) — e.g. a comparison like CPU below 90, or is_state(...). It's re-checked when referenced entities change. An empty/unknown result or a render error counts as unknown (no false alarm). Point it at the guarded device — don't reference this guard's own status/health entities, or you create a feedback loop.

assigned_device.name: Assigned device (optional)
assigned_device.description: Attaches Necromancer's status entities to an existing device.

strategy.title: Recovery strategy
strategy.description: How should the device be brought back when it fails? On the next screen a health-check toggle (on by default) decides whether the fix is verified against the device's health before going quiet; with it off, the action is assumed to have worked.

switch.title: Power-cycle a switch
switch.description: Powers a switch off and on when the device fails. The timing settings below set how long to wait before reacting and how long to pause afterwards; with a health-check, also how long to wait for the device to recover and how often to retry.

data.switch_entity: Switch to power off and on (e.g. a smart plug)

behavior.name: Behaviour
behavior.description: When Necromancer reacts, and — with a health-check — how often it retries before giving up. The defaults are usually fine.

data.boot_window: Wait until the device is healthy again

notification.name: Notification
notification.description: Optional — run an action when this guard hits a problem, recovery or escalation. You provide the action (a phone push, a TTS announcement, a light, a script — anything), and Necromancer passes the alert text as variables, so you decide whether and how to be told. Take the ready-made `message`, or build your own from `name`, `event_text` (the line without the name) and `event`; `attempt`, `max`, `attempts` and `reason` are available where they apply. Reference a variable with double curly braces in your action.

linked_guards.name: Linked guards
linked_guards.description: Group this guard with other recover guards. When any guard in the group repairs, the others follow — they wait and re-check afterwards instead of repairing in parallel. Linking is mutual and transitive: link to a guard that already has links and you join its whole group; to leave, clear all of them.

data.notify_follower_success: Report success even when this guard follows a group repair

reload.name: Restart device integration
reload.description: After a repair attempt, reload the assigned device's integration before checking its health again — useful when Home Assistant must reconnect to the device.

data.reload_entry: Restart the assigned device's integration after a repair

action.title: Run an action
action.description: Runs your action when the device fails. The timing settings below set how long to wait before reacting and how long to pause afterwards; with a health-check, also how long to wait for the device to recover and how often to retry.

recovery_action.name: Recovery action
recovery_action.description: What to run to bring the device back. An action can be anything Home Assistant can do — a service or script call, an SSH/`shell_command`, a `rest_command` or webhook, or a multi-step sequence with delays, waits and conditions. It runs blocking; with the health-check variant Necromancer then waits (up to the boot window) for the device to report OK again.

data.action: Action (e.g. a script, service, SSH, webhook)

behavior.name: Behaviour
behavior.description: When Necromancer reacts, and — with a health-check — how often it retries before giving up. The defaults are usually fine.

data.boot_window: Wait until the device is healthy again

notification.name: Notification
notification.description: Optional — run an action when this guard hits a problem, recovery or escalation. You provide the action (a phone push, a TTS announcement, a light, a script — anything), and Necromancer passes the alert text as variables, so you decide whether and how to be told. Take the ready-made `message`, or build your own from `name`, `event_text` (the line without the name) and `event`; `attempt`, `max`, `attempts` and `reason` are available where they apply. Reference a variable with double curly braces in your action.

linked_guards.name: Linked guards
linked_guards.description: Group this guard with other recover guards. When any guard in the group repairs, the others follow — they wait and re-check afterwards instead of repairing in parallel. Linking is mutual and transitive: link to a guard that already has links and you join its whole group; to leave, clear all of them.

data.notify_follower_success: Report success even when this guard follows a group repair

reload.name: Restart device integration
reload.description: After a repair attempt, reload the assigned device's integration before checking its health again — useful when Home Assistant must reconnect to the device.

data.reload_entry: Restart the assigned device's integration after a repair

actions.title: Off/on actions
actions.description: Runs the off action, waits, then the on action when the device fails. The timing settings below set how long to wait before reacting and how long to pause afterwards; with a health-check, also how long to wait for the device to recover and how often to retry.

recovery_action.name: Recovery actions
recovery_action.description: Two sequences with a wait between: the off action (cut power / turn off), then the on action (restore / turn on). Each can be any Home Assistant action — service or script, SSH, `rest_command`, webhook, sequence. Variables you set in the off action — plus `attempt` and `max` — are readable in the on action, so you can capture state before the cut and restore it afterwards, no helper entity needed.

data.off_action: Off action (cut power / turn the device off)
data.on_action: On action (restore power / turn the device on)

behavior.name: Behaviour
behavior.description: When Necromancer reacts, and — with a health-check — how often it retries before giving up. The defaults are usually fine.

data.boot_window: Wait until the device is healthy again

notification.name: Notification
notification.description: Optional — run an action when this guard hits a problem, recovery or escalation. You provide the action (a phone push, a TTS announcement, a light, a script — anything), and Necromancer passes the alert text as variables, so you decide whether and how to be told. Take the ready-made `message`, or build your own from `name`, `event_text` (the line without the name) and `event`; `attempt`, `max`, `attempts` and `reason` are available where they apply. Reference a variable with double curly braces in your action.

Linking is mutual and transitive: link to a guard that already has links and you join its whole group; to leave, clear all of them.

reload.description: After a repair attempt, reload the assigned device's integration before checking its health again — useful when Home Assistant must reconnect to the device.

data.reload_entry: Restart the assigned device's integration after a repair

poe_port.title: Auto-PoE — find the port
poe_port.description: Finds the matching PoE port by the device's id and powers it off and on. The timing settings below set how long to wait before reacting and how long to pause afterwards; with a health-check, also how long to wait for the device to recover and how often to retry. Manage the ports under the integration's "Configure".

data.expected_id: Device id (e.g. MAC, IP or name)

behavior.name: Behaviour
behavior.description: When Necromancer reacts, and — with a health-check — how often it retries before giving up. The defaults are usually fine.

data.boot_window: Wait until the device is healthy again

notification.name: Notification
notification.description: Optional — run an action when this guard hits a problem, recovery or escalation. You provide the action (a phone push, a TTS announcement, a light, a script — anything), and Necromancer passes the alert text as variables, so you decide whether and how to be told. Take the ready-made `message`, or build your own from `name`, `event_text` (the line without the name) and `event`; `attempt`, `max`, `attempts` and `reason` are available where they apply. Reference a variable with double curly braces in your action.

linked_guards.description: Group this guard with other recover guards. When any guard in the group repairs, the others follow — they wait and re-check afterwards instead of repairing in parallel. Linking is mutual and transitive: link to a guard that already has links and you join its whole group; to leave, clear all of them.

reload.name: Restart device integration
reload.description: After a repair attempt, reload the assigned device's integration before checking its health again — useful when Home Assistant must reconnect to the device.

data.reload_entry: Restart the assigned device's integration after a repair

notify.title: Notification
notify.description: What should happen when a problem is detected.

notification.name: Notification
notification.description: Optional — run an action when this guard hits a problem, recovery or escalation. You provide the action (a phone push, a TTS announcement, a light, a script — anything), and Necromancer passes the alert text as variables, so you decide whether and how to be told. Take the ready-made `message`, or build your own from `name`, `event_text` (the line without the name) and `event`; `attempt`, `max`, `attempts` and `reason` are available where they apply. Reference a variable with double curly braces in your action.

repair_poe_port.name: Repair PoE port
repair_poe_port.description: Resolve a device id to its PoE port and power-cycle it. Blocking and serialised per port, so multiple guards or automations coordinate on a single cycle instead of fighting over the same port.

id.name: Device id
id.description: The id that identifies the device on its PoE port — the MAC, IP or static label configured for that port.

reset.name: Reset
reset.description: Clear an escalated guard back to normal and re-check it. If the device is still unhealthy the guard tries to recover again; if it has recovered it simply resumes watching.

snooze.name: Snooze
snooze.description: Temporarily suspend a guard for a set duration — it ignores the device's health and raises no alerts (e.g. during planned maintenance), then resumes automatically.

duration.name: Duration
duration.description: How long to suspend the guard before it automatically resumes.

unsnooze.name: Unsnooze
unsnooze.description: Resume a snoozed guard immediately instead of waiting for its snooze to elapse.

notify_guard.name: Notify via guard
notify_guard.description: Send a custom message through a guard's own notify action — for recovery scripts that want to report their own progress on the same channel the guard already uses.

message.name: Message
message.description: The text to send. Exposed to the notify action as the message and event_text variables; the guard name is the title.

event.name: Event key
event.description: Optional routing key exposed as the event variable (default: custom), so your notify action can branch on it.

event_text.name: Event text
event_text.description: Optional name-less text exposed as event_text (defaults to the message) — for titles/TTS that set the name separately.

attempt.name: Attempt
attempt.description: Optional attempt count exposed as attempt; also derives the plural-correct attempts phrase.

max.name: Max attempts
max.description: Optional attempt limit exposed as max.

snooze_all.name: Snooze all
snooze_all.description: Snooze every guard for a duration — system-wide maintenance mode. Each guard ignores health and raises no alerts, then auto-resumes. Guards mid-recovery are skipped (best-effort).

duration.name: Duration
duration.description: How long to suspend all guards before they auto-resume.

check_health.name: Check health
check_health.description: Evaluate a guard's health right now and return it (ok / unhealthy / unknown) — for a recovery script to re-use the guard's own health-check instead of rebuilding it.

guard.name: Guard
guard.description: The guard's status entity — the value a recovery action receives as guard_entity_id.

wait_for_health.name: Wait for health
wait_for_health.description: Wait until a guard reports healthy again, or the timeout elapses. Returns health, timed_out and waited_s — so a staged recovery can verify between tiers using the guard's own health-check.

guard.name: Guard
guard.description: The guard's status entity — the value a recovery action receives as guard_entity_id.

timeout.name: Timeout
timeout.description: Seconds to wait for health to read OK. Defaults to the guard's boot window.

check_first.name: Check first
check_first.description: Evaluate once before waiting and return immediately if already healthy (default: on).

snooze_during_recovery.message: Cannot snooze {name} while it is actively recovering — wait for the recovery to finish.

unknown_guard.message: {entity_id} is not a Necromancer guard status entity — pass the guard's status sensor (sensor.<name>_status, the guard_entity_id a recovery action receives).
